original_abstract: This research investigates the proficiency of large language models (LLMs) in representing geometric data and their associated spatial relationships. Utilizing models such as GPT-2 and BERT, we encode geometries provided in Well-Known Text (WKT) format and process the resulting embeddings through downstream classifiers and regressors. This framework allows us to evaluate how effectively LLM-generated representations capture fundamental geometric attributes. Our experimental results indicate that while these embeddings successfully preserve geometry types and identify certain spatial relations—achieving up to 73% accuracy—significant limitations persist in estimating precise numeric values and retrieving spatially related objects. These findings underscore a critical gap in the ability of current foundation models to grasp the nuances of geospatial data. We conclude that future GeoAI applications require more robust integration of domain-specific knowledge to better handle the complexities inherent in spatial reasoning.
humanized_abstract: The ability of large language models (LLMs) to represent geometric concepts and spatial relationships has not yet been well explored. In this research, we leveraged the capabilities of two LLMs, namely GPT-2 and BERT, to discover the representational capabilities of such models for geometric data and relationships. We used different architectures of classifiers and regressors to test the abilities of the embeddings obtained from the input of Well-Known Text (WKT) descriptions of geometries. Our experiments showed that the embeddings are able to well preserve the properties of the input geometric data up to the level of accuracy of 73% and represent some spatial relationships. However, many aspects including regression of exact numerical values and spatial search of geometric data related to query data remain to be addressed by GeoAI in the coming future, which calls for better knowledge incorporation.
